children.name: Psophia
children.description: Medium-sized, long-legged terrestrial birds with a distinctive humped back, soft plumage and a low, booming call from which their common name derives. They are restricted to the Amazon basin and are important seed dispersers in the rainforest ecosystem.

children.name: Psophia crepitans
children.description: The most widespread trumpeter, ranging across the Guiana Shield and the upper Amazon in Colombia, Venezuela, Brazil and Peru. It has a black body, a greenish-purple iridescent lower back and distinctive silver-grey wing coverts. Its deep, resonant 'boom' is amplified by a modified trachea and can be heard for over a kilometre.

children.name: Psophia viridis
children.description: Endemic to the Brazilian Amazon south of the Amazon River, this species has dark olive-green wing coverts that distinguish it from its grey-winged relative. It is a social bird that travels in groups of up to a dozen individuals through the terra firme forest, following army ant swarms to capture fleeing insects.

children.name: Psophia leucoptera
children.description: Restricted to the western and southern Amazon south of the Rio Negro, this species has whitish-yellow wing coverts. It is the least studied of the trumpeters but shares the same terrestrial, frugivorous habits. Flocks are semi-nomadic within their home range, following the seasonal fruiting of key rainforest tree species.
